---DESIGN.md head---
# DDx Website Design System

Design tokens for the DDx microsite. All tokens are defined in
`assets/css/custom.css` and flow into the Hugo build via the pipeline:

```
resources.Get "css/custom.css" | minify | fingerprint
```

The fingerprinted stylesheet is linked in `layouts/index.html`:
```go-html-template
{{- $css := resources.Get "css/custom.css" | minify | fingerprint -}}
<link rel="stylesheet" href="{{ $css.RelPermalink }}">
```

---

## Palette — lever / load / fulcrum

The DDx color system uses a mechanical metaphor: **lever** (primary action),
**load** (supporting tint), **fulcrum** (anchor/dark), **brass** (warm accent).

| Token | Light | Dark | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| `--ddx-accent-lever` | `#3B5B7A` | `#3B5B7A` | Primary links, CTAs, card numbers |
| `--ddx-accent-load` | `#5A85B0` | `#5A85B0` | Secondary card accent |
| `--ddx-accent-fulcrum` | `#2A3F56` | `#2A3F56` | Tertiary card accent, hover state |
| `--ddx-accent-brass` | `#9A6A2F` | `#C79B5B` | Kicker labels, quote border, action underline |

Hextra's primary tint scale is driven by:

```css
--primary-hue: 210deg;
--primary-saturation: 36%;
--primary-lightness: 35%;   /* 55% in dark mode for legibility */
```

This re-tints Hextra's link, focus-ring, and badge utilities to the lever color
without touching the theme source.

---

## Surface & Foreground

| Token | Light | Dark | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| `--ddx-bg-canvas` | `#F7F1E7` | `#0F1117` | Page background |
| `--ddx-bg-surface` | `#FFFDF8` | `#1A1E2A` | Card/panel background |
| `--ddx-fg-ink` | `#17130F` | `#E8EAF0` | Body text |
| `--ddx-fg-muted` | `#675F54` | `#9CA3AF` | Secondary text, captions |

1 {{- $css := resources.Get "css/custom.css" | minify | fingerprint -}} 2 3 4 5 6 7 {{ site.Title }} 8 9 10 19 20 21

41

Document-driven AI development

42

Documents drive the agents. DDx drives the documents.

43

44 AI agents write code fast. DDx keeps the work structured — every task 45 has acceptance criteria, every run executes in isolation, and every 46 result leaves auditable evidence behind. 47

48 49
50 Get Started 51 See Features 52
53
54 55
56 
brew install ddx
57	ddx init
58	ddx install helix
59	ddx work
60

HELIX is the opinionated workflow that runs on top of DDx — phases, gates, and bounded actions for teams that want a methodology out of the box.

61
62
63 64 65
66

The problem

67
68

AI agents can write code. They cannot hold context across a codebase, a sprint, or a team.

69

Without structure, agents drift — regenerating work, ignoring dependencies, reverting to bad states. DDx gives agents a contract to execute against: acceptance criteria on every task, specs that encode intent, and evidence that makes every run inspectable.

70
71
72 73 74
75

How it works

76
77
78 01 79

Define the work

80

Each task declares acceptance criteria and its dependencies upfront. Nothing runs without a contract.

81
82
83 02 84

Execute in isolation

85

Agents run in dedicated git worktrees — no shared state, no context bleed. Results merge cleanly or are preserved for review.

86
87
88 03 89

Review against criteria

90

Every run is graded against its acceptance criteria. Work that passes closes. Work that fails re-queues with the findings attached.

91
92
93
94 95 96
97
98

Capabilities

99 All features → 100
101
102
103 01 104
105

Structured Work Queue

106

Tasks carry acceptance criteria, dependency graphs, and a full history of every agent attempt.

107
108
109
110 02 111
112

Isolated Execution

113

Every task runs in its own git worktree. Inputs, outputs, cost, and diffs are captured automatically.

114
115
116
117 03 118
119

Installable Workflows

120

One command adds a full methodology to your project. HELIX is the first. Build your own or share one.

121
122
123
124 04 125
126

MCP Access

127

Expose your work queue and documents over MCP so any agent or supervisor can observe and steer.

128
129
130
131
132 133 134
135

Principles

136
137
138 01 139
140

Platform, Not Methodology

141

DDx ships primitives — beads, agent dispatch, document library. Workflow opinions live in plugins like HELIX, never in the core.

142
143
144
145 02 146
147

Project-Local by Default

148

Every plugin, persona, and bead lives under the project root. No global state, no machine-wide side effects, no surprises.

149
150
151
152 03 153
154

Validate Your Work

155

Every bead carries acceptance criteria. Every agent run leaves evidence. Reviews gate close — failing beads re-queue with findings.

156
157
158
159 04 160
161

Evidence Over Promises

162

Each agent invocation writes inputs, outputs, and cost into the execution bundle. You can always tell which model produced which diff.

163
164
165
166 05 167
168

Cost-Tiered Routing

169

Cheap models do routine work, strong models review, deterministic checks sit at the top of the ladder. Optimize closed-work-per-dollar.

170
171
172
173 06 174
175

Shared Library, Not Silos

176

Patterns, prompts, and personas flow back to the master library via git subtree. What works in one repo reaches the next.

177

See it in action

1	---
2	title: Features
3	description: "DDx platform capabilities — stable, beta, and planned."
4	---
5	
6	DDx provides a focused set of platform primitives for AI-assisted development. Each capability is listed with its current maturity level.
7	
8	## Core Platform
9	
10	### Bead Tracker {{< maturity "stable" >}}
11	
12	Beads are the atomic unit of work in DDx. Each bead carries a title, description, acceptance criteria, dependency graph, and full state history. The `ddx bead` subcommands cover the complete CRUD lifecycle.
13	
14	```
15	ddx bead create "Add login endpoint" --ac "returns 200 on valid credentials"
16	ddx bead list --status ready
17	ddx bead close <id>
18	```
19	
20	### Agent Dispatch {{< maturity "stable" >}}
21	
22	Invoke AI agents through a single interface. DDx selects the configured harness, passes the prompt and context, and records token usage and cost per run.
23	
24	```
25	ddx agent run --harness claude --prompt prompts/implement.md
26	ddx agent execute-bead <id>
27	```
28	
29	### Execution Engine {{< maturity "stable" >}}
30	
31	Each bead executes in an isolated git worktree. Evidence is captured after every run. Passing beads are merged back; timed-out or failing runs are preserved for inspection.
32	
33	```
34	ddx agent execute-loop          # drain the queue
35	ddx agent execute-bead <id> --from HEAD
36	```
37	
38	### Plugin System {{< maturity "stable" >}}
39	
40	Workflow methodologies are packaged as plugins. One command installs a plugin under the project root — no global state, no machine-wide side effects.
41	
42	```
43	ddx install helix               # install HELIX workflow
44	ddx install ddx                 # update DDx default resources
45	```
46	
47	### Document Library {{< maturity "stable" >}}
48	
49	Prompts, patterns, templates, and personas live in versioned library directories. The library ships with DDx and is extended by plugins.
50	
51	```
52	ddx prompts list
53	ddx templates apply scaffold
54	ddx persona list
55	```
56	
57	### Persona System {{< maturity "stable" >}}
58	
59	Personas are reusable AI personality templates — code-reviewer, test-engineer, architect. Projects bind personas to roles in `.ddx.yml`. Every agent run picks up the bound persona automatically.
60	
61	```
62	ddx persona show code-reviewer
63	ddx persona bind code-reviewer --role reviewer
64	```
65	
66	### Git Sync {{< maturity "stable" >}}
67	
68	DDx resources are versioned via git subtree. `ddx update` pulls the latest library from the master repository; `ddx contribute` pushes improvements back.
69	
70	```
71	ddx update
72	ddx contribute
73	```
74	
75	## Extended Capabilities
76	
77	### MCP Server {{< maturity "beta" >}}
78	
79	DDx can expose beads, documents, and execution history over the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and HTTP. Remote supervisors — including Claude Desktop and compatible clients — can query work state and steer execution.
80	
81	```
82	ddx server start
83	```
84	
85	### Web UI {{< maturity "beta" >}}
86	
87	A browser interface for the bead tracker and document library. Built with SvelteKit and embedded in the `ddx-server` binary. Useful for reviewing bead state, execution history, and document relationships without leaving the browser.
88	
89	### Cost-Tiered Routing {{< maturity "beta" >}}
90	
91	Configure multiple model endpoints with tier labels. DDx routes beads to the cheapest model that meets the complexity threshold, escalating on failure. Optimize closed-work-per-dollar across Claude, Codex, Gemini, and local models.
92	
93	## Planned
94	
95	### Remote Execution {{< maturity "planned" >}}
96	
97	Run beads on remote compute — a cloud VM, a CI runner, or a dedicated agent host — without changing the local workflow. The bead queue and merge result flow stay identical.
98	
99	### Team Sync {{< maturity "planned" >}}
100	
101	Shared bead queues across a team. Agents on multiple machines claim, execute, and close beads from the same queue without coordination overhead.
102

45	  test('homepage screenshot', async ({ page }) => {
